issues.title: OAuth as an authorization delegation framework
issues.author: PieterKas
issues.body:
>{{RFC6749}} is an established and maintained framework of specifications for requesting, acquiring, and proving ownership of pieces of authorization in the form of bearer tokens.

I think we should enforce that OAuth is a authorization delegation framework where the RO delegates access to the Client to access and API on the RS by calling the AS (we can split mechanics). "pieces od the authorization" = "authorization delegation by the user". It hink it is fine to also point out that additional this does not constitute all the authorization information.

issues.title: Transacction tokens
issues.author: PieterKas
issues.body:
>{{OAuth.TRAT}} is new specification for formattting pieces of authorization in the form of transaction bound bearer tokens. 

How about this: 

{{OAuth.TRAT}}  defines short-lived, cryptographically protected tokens that carry and preserve a requester’s identity and authorization context across all internal service calls in a trusted domain during an external API-initiated transaction. It allows for downscoping access and binding authorisation data to a specific transaction, reducing the risks of token theft and replay attacks.

issues.title: Clarify Client
issues.author: PieterKas

The term client feels overloaded (as the AI agent can itself be an OAuth client). Perhaps we need to talk about OAuth Clients vs regular "clients". Some clever languagge need here (or we need to be explicit about the kind of client to avoid confusion)

The original proposal had a "user" in the place where we now have "client" - I would suggest we go back to that terminology given how overloaded the client term is. That way we can talk about the user delegating authorisation to the agent using OAuth. Right now we are mixing protocol semantics (Oauth client) with system semantics (users, agents, LLM, Tools etc).

issues.title: Confusing order of functions for AIMS
issues.author: identitymonk
issues.body:
The current draft establish the order has:

- Agent Identifiers: Unique identifier assigned to every Agent.
- Agent Credentials: Cryptographic binding between the Agent Identifier and attributes of the Agent.
- Agent Attestation: Mechanisms for determining and assigning the identifier and issue credentials based on measurements of the Agent's environment.
- Agent Credential Provisioning: The mechanism for provisioning credentials to the agent at runtime.

1. Credentials here is not a function but an outcome - We could talk about credentialling but then it would collide with the definition is Attestation and Provisioning
2. Same for identifier: It is an outcome not an action - We could talk about Identification
3. Attestation and Provisioning are both talking about issuing the credentials - I think it should be only provisioning  that should have this function and definition
4. Attestation should be before identifier - I attest this workload is of type A therefore I create an identifier an identifier.


I propose:

~~~aasvg
+--------------+----------------------------------+--------------+
|    Policy    |   Monitoring & Remediation       |  Compliance  |
|              +----------------------------------+              |
|              |          Authorization           |              |
|              +----------------------------------+              |
|              |          Authentication          |              |
|              +----------------------------------+              |
|              |          Credentialling          |              |
|              +----------------------------------+              |
|              |          Identification          |              |
|              +----------------------------------+              |
|              |           Attestation            |              |
+--------------+----------------------------------+--------------+
~~~

> note: there is a typo on compliance

comments.author: PieterKas
comments.body:
I think this revised diagram misses the point for creating the original diagram to start with. The original diagram is a way to create a mental map for people on how to combine the existing standards as building blocks to create an AIMS system. The above diagram is not helping with this (I am not aware of identification specs, or credentialing specs...). When you build a system you don't build attestation first, you pick how you will identify the components first.

The original diagram is about creating a mental model for the building blocks needed to create a AI identity management system, and the ordering is about the sequence in which those building blocks get used. You have to decide you identifier format, then you have to decide how he identifier is bound to other attributes (credentials), then you have to decide how you will do identity proofing (attestation), Once you know how you do those things, you have to provision the credentials, now that the workload has. credentials, they can be used for authentication, once you can authenticate, you can authorize, Once you authorize, wou should monitor and remediate the system. There are standards for all of those things and the current diagram is intended to give people an idea on how to combine the existing specs and stack these blocks.

I think what may be needed is more text to explain how to think about this framework as building blocks, as well as a series of design decisions.

You also gave me another idea. Waht if we use "identity proofing" instead of "attestation"...

comments.body: Credential validation tends to be a function of the authentication protocols - e.g. when using MTLS, we point to the relevant specs, which in turn is very specific about how a client cert should be validated. I don't think we want to talk about credential validation in the credentials section of the document, but instead have it inferred via the authentication section (and other palces where the credential may be used).

issues.title: Historic Detailed Section
issues.author: PieterKas
issues.body:
Copy of text removed from draft prior to IETF 125 - to be re-introduced at a future date.

-----------------------End of "High Level Map"------------------------------

-----------------------Early Thoughts on Detailed Spec Below------------------------------

# Agent Authorization - Next Level Detail

During Agent execution, authorization must be enforced at all the components involved in the process to provide an in-depth protection of the resources that might be interacted with. For each component, we must consider the following 3 phases:
- Negotiation between the component and its caller on the required pieces of authorization required to interact with the component
- Acquisition of the piece of authorization by the caller at the authorization server authoritative for the component it wants to communication
- Validation of the piece of authorization in the context of the request by the component

Those phases rely on the following standards for enforcement of the access control:
- {{OAUTH-FRAMEWORK}} is an established and maintained framework of specifications for requesting, acquiring, and proving ownership of pieces of authorization in the form of bearer tokens.
- {{OpenIDConnect.AuthZEN}} is a new specification for exchanging authorization requests and decisions between the layer acting at the Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) and a Policy Decision Point (PDP).
- {{OAUTH-TRANS-TOKENS}} is new specification for formatting pieces of authorization in the form of transaction bound bearer tokens.

~~~ ascii-art

                       | Large Language |
                       |   Model (LLM)  |
                       +----------------+
                              ▲   |
                              │   |               ┌(H)┐
                              |   ▼               ▼   │
+--------------+         +------------+         +-------------+         +-----------+
|  User /      |─(A)(C)─►|  AI Agent  |─(E)(H)─►| Agent /     |─(J)(M)─►| Services  |
|      /       |         | (workload) |         |      /      |         |   and     |
|     / System |◄────────|            |◄────────|     / Tools |◄────────| Resources |
+--------------+         +------------+         +-------------+         +-----------+
     ▲                       ▲  ▲                   ▲   ▲                    ▲
     |    ┌──(F)─────────────┘  |                   |   |                    |
     |    |   ┌───(D)───────────┘                  (I)  |                    |
     |    |   |  +---------------+                  |  (K)                  (N)
    (B)   |   |  |    Policy     |                  |   |                    |
     |    |   └─►|   Decision    |◄─────────────────┘   |                    |
     |    |      |    Point      |◄─────────────────────┼────────────────────┘
     |    |      +---------------+                      |
     |    |             ▲                               |
     |    |          (G)(L)                             |
     |    |             ▼                               |
     |    |      +---------------+                      |
     |    └─────►| Authorization |◄─────────────────────┘
     └──────────►|   server      |
                 +---------------+
~~~


> Key point - OAuth is broadly supported and provides a delegation model for users to clients (aka Agents). Agents can obtain access tokens directly (client credentials flow, using above authentication methods) or it can be delegated to them by a user (OAuth flows). Make point that the access token includes the client_id, which is the same as the Agent ID (ore related to it) and can be used for authorization decisions, along with other claims in an Access Token (reference JWT Access token spec). Make provision for opaque tokens as well. Discuss Downscoping of Agent authorization using transaction tokens. Discuss cross-domain authorization (use cases) and how it may be achieved (identity chaining and cross-domain authorization). Discuss human in the loop authorization. Note concerns, refer to cross-device BCP as examples of consent phishing attacks. Talk about CIBA as a protocol.

## System to AI Agent
### (A) Negotiation - OPTIONAL

#### Flow

Following {{OAUTH-RESOURCE-METADATA}}, the System MUST act as an OAuth2 client. It MUST interact with the AI Agent on its metadata endpoint which MUST be an OAuth 2.0 protected resource as defined by {{OAUTH-RESOURCE-METADATA}}.

The System will then understand which Authorization Server MUST be the authority this AI Agent; which scopes or authorization details values MAY be required to access this AI Agent; and if extension mechanisms MAY be required to fulfil.

#### Security

The System MUST follow the best current practices described in {{OAUTH-BCP}}.

### (B) Initial Authorization

Based on the information collected as part of (A) or based on its configuration, the System MUST initiate an authorization request to the Authorization Server acting as authority for the AI Agent.

#### Flow

For this, the System MUST use one of the grant types described in {{OAUTH-FRAMEWORK}} as follows:

- In a case of the System is acting on its behalf, it MUST start a client credential grant flow as described in {{Section 1.3.4 of OAUTH-FRAMEWORK}};
- In the case of the System is acting on behalf of a user:
  - If the System can interact directly with the user (e.g., browser-based), the System MUST use the authorization code grant flow as described in {{Section 4.1 of OAUTH-FRAMEWORK}};
  - If the System has limited input capabilities, but the User is present at the device, the System MUST use the Device Authorization Grant {{RFC8628}};
  - If the User is not present at the consuming device (decoupled flow), the System MUST use Client-Initiated Backchannel Authentication {{OpenIDConnect.CIBA}}.

#### Extensions

As part of the grant flow, the User / System MUST also fulfill all the expected extensions it has an understanding of. For example, not exhaustively:
- Binding the requested tokens to a possessed key as defined in {{RFC9449}} or {#wpt}
- Requesting additional authorization details as defined in {{RFC9396}} or {{RFC9126}}

#### Identification

The System MUST act as an OAuth2 Client which MAY be assigned a WIMSE identifier, which MAY be a SPIFFE ID.

If so, the System MUST provide a way to resolve one value to the other, either through:
- A metadata document URL used OAuth2 Client identifier as defined in {{OAUTH-CLIENT-METADATA}};
- Metadata information provided as part of the Private Key JWT OAuth client credential as defined in {{OAUTH-CLIENTAUTH-JWT}}
- Public Certificate information provided as part of the mutual TLS OAuth client credential as defined in {{OAuth.mTLS.Auth-RFC8705}}

#### Authentication

The System MUST authentication using either:
- Private Key JWT OAuth client authentication as defined in {{OAUTH-CLIENTAUTH-JWT}}
- Mutual TLS OAuth client authentication as defined in {{OAuth.mTLS.Auth-RFC8705}}

#### Security

The System MUST follow the best current practices described in {{OAUTH-BCP}} for the interactions with the AI Agent and {{RFC8725}} when handling JWTs.

### (C) Access to the AI Agent

#### Flow

To access an AI Agent, the System MUST present its authorization credential as defined in {{Section 7 of OAUTH-FRAMEWORK}} as well as defined in {{RFC6750}}. The client MUST be able to process error codes as defined in section 3 of {{RFC6750}}.

The AI Agent MAY request additional details to be provided through a new authorization credentials using {{OAuth.step-up.Auth-RFC9470}} or error codes as defined in {{RFC6750}}.

#### Security

The client MUST follow the best current practices described in {{OAUTH-BCP}}.

### (D) Controlling access to the AI Agent

#### Flow

The AI Agent MUST validate the presented token as described in {{OAUTH-FRAMEWORK}} as well as defined in {{RFC6750}}.

If token introspection is required, the AI Agent MUST follow {{OAUTH-TOKEN-INTROSPECTION}} as well as {{RFC9701}}.

If the provided token is a JWT profiled token as defined in {{OAUTH-ACCESSTOKEN-JWT}}, the AI Agent MUST follow the section 2 of this specification as part of the token validation.

If the AI Agent delegates its access control logic to a Policy decision point, it MUST follow {{OpenIDConnect.AuthZEN}} specification for requesting and receiving a decision for the access.

#### Security

TODO

## AI Agent To Other Agent / Tools

Interactions between an AI Agent and Tools are globally specified by {{MCP}}. Those sections only focus on the Identification, authentication, and authorization aspects of the specification.

Interactions between an AI Agent and other AI Agents are globally specified by {{A2A}}. Note that derived specifications and domain specific specification have emerged like {{AP2}} for Agent payment interaction, {{ACP}} for Agent to commerce flows. Those sections only focus on the Identification, authentication, and authorization aspects of those specifications.

### (E) Negotiation

#### Case of a Tool
Following {{MCP}}, the AI Agent MUST interact with Tools on the metadata endpoint of an OAuth 2.0 protected resource to understand which Authorization Server is the authority this resource; which scopes or authorization details values MAY be required to access this resources; and if proof of possession needs to be presented as standardized with {{RFC9449}}.

#### Case of an Agent

Following {{A2A}}, the AI Agent MUST interact with the other Agent through their Agent Card. If Extended Agent Card is implemented, the calling AI Agent MUST collect the information to understand which Authorization Server is the authority this resource; which scopes or authorization details values MAY be required to access this resources; and if proof of possession needs to be presented as standardized with {{RFC9449}}.

{{ACP}} and {{AP2}} Agents are expected to follow the same Agent Card feature as {{A2A}} Agents.

### (F) AI Agent Authorization

Based on the information collected as part of (E), the AI Agent is initiating an authorization request with the Authorization Server acting as authority for the AI Tools.

Such authorization request can allow to down, change or translate scope; enrich the authorization with new details based on the context; extend the time boundaries of the authorization; or change from a delegation mode to an impersonation mode and reversely.

- If the AI Agent acts on its behalf as a system:
  - If it does not want or need to refer to the previous context, it MUST start a client credential grant flow as described in {{Section 1.3.4 of OAUTH-FRAMEWORK}};
  - If it does want or need to refer to the previous context, it MUST start a token exchange flow as described in {{OAUTH-TOKEN-EXCHANGE}}. The AI Agent will be able to decide in between obtaining tokens representing a Delegation or an Impersonation as described in section 1.1 of the specification.
- If the AI Agent acts on delegation by a user:
  - If the AI Agent can interact and want to interact with the user through the client, it MUST start an authorization code grant flow as described in {{Section 1.3.1 of OAUTH-FRAMEWORK}};
  - If the AI Agent cannot or does not want to interact with the user through the client, it MAY:
    - Start a token exchange flow as described in {{OAUTH-TOKEN-EXCHANGE}}. The AI Agent will be able to decide in between obtaining tokens representing a Delegation or an Impersonation as described in section 1.1 of the specification.
    - Start a client initated backchannel authorized request as described in {{OpenIDConnect.CIBA}}

If the AI Agent knows that the underlying actions

If the metadata documents are cryptographically signed, the AI Agent MUST validate the signature before using the information for any authentication and authorization decision.

issues.title: Telling an agent from a non-agent
issues.author: PieterKas
issues.body:
How to distinguish between an AI Agent and a regular Oauth client. Some feedback that the Microsoft model makes it explicit  through claims, that say "this is an agent" or "this is an agent acting obo"

My mental model for this (could be wrong) is that the RS andAS needs to know that certain identifiers are assigned to agents and treat them differently. The claim name is mostly orthogonal to this, even if there was a separate claim name for agents, you would still need to know which agent and how to treat it differently when making authorisation decisions at the AS or RS.

The AS and RS will both have to know which identifiers are assigned to agents. This is needed, regardless of which claim is used to express the identifier. Sometimes the client_id is associated with an agent, sometimes not. 

The AS can manage this via registration (e.g. could be metadata in CIMD) and aplpy different policies when issuing access tokens (more restricted, narrower scopes, shorter lived). The RS could use the same mechanism (with caching and so forth) and apply additional restrictions. I can also imagine the actual identifiers in either the credential used to authenticate or in the client_id being structured in a specific way (works inside an enterprise or closed eco-system, needs standard to do it more broadly).

The messy bit is that in an agentic world the AS and the RS needs to make decisions on the workload/client/agent identifier, which they did not have to do in the past, regardless of the claim name used. If using a different claim name is used as a logic switch in validation logic, that can also be achieved by just having a claim that says "agent=true" in the token (or the metadata). 

It may be useful to point which mechanisms needs to be in place to tell if a client is and agent, or just a client (goes a little against the AI is just a workload...)

comments.author: identitymonk
comments.body:
CIMD is now the norm. If more information needs to be provided about the nature of the client, it should come from there, up to the RS to do the look-up and retrieve the appropriate information.

Risk, from a RS perspective, will be far more than attribute = 1 or 0. There is far more to the question of: "Are you an agent?" than just question. Like:
- Are a sketchy, side of table developed or enterprise grade agent?
- What are you composed of?
- Can you provide an attestation of your structure?

All those questions send back to the notion of "Workload ⊃ AI Agent".

Will the next question be "how do you prove you are a MCP server access me RS"? We cannot add signals in token about all and everything

Agent workloads require explicit security classification, but they do not acquire legal personhood.
This distinction must remain clear in the specification.

Authorization systems may evaluate workload/client/agent identity for execution risk controls (scope, TTL, step-up, attestation, replay limits), while legal authority remains bound to a human or legal entity principal and expressed through bounded delegation.

The draft should avoid any framing that implies agents hold independent legal standing.
Agents execute under delegated authority; they do not originate legal authority.

comments.author: kevin-biot
comments.body:
> The MCP/RS interaction is out of scope for this issue (which is scoped to distinguishing between the agent and non agent clients). For the RS/MCP use case - that feels like a different issue - best to open a new issue for that so we can discuss it separately from this issue.

I agree MCP/RS specifics should be tracked in a separate issue from “agent vs non-agent client classification.”

That said, I think the draft should include a clear security boundary note:

- Internal tool-orchestration architectures (including MCP-like patterns) are out of scope for this document.
- However, protocol-level agent authentication/authorization does not, by itself, guarantee safe tool execution.
- Deployments should enforce a deterministic execution boundary between model output and effectful tool actions.
- Externally visible state changes should require independent authorization checks at execution time, not only trust in LLM output.

In other words: this draft can standardize identity/delegation interoperability across components, but it should not be interpreted as solving internal agent-to-tool safety by itself.

Context: we use internal permit/warrant mechanisms in our implementation, but this comment is not proposing those mechanisms as standards. They are our internal solution ;-)

What we are contributing here is only the interoperability requirement:

1. There must be an execution-time authorization boundary between model output and effectful tool actions.
2. Tool invocation authority must be explicit, bounded, and short-lived (intent/action, audience/resource, scope, actor context, time bounds).
3. Authorization must be re-evaluated at execution time for each effectful step (not implied globally by session auth).
4. Missing or inconsistent authorization context must fail closed.
5. The specific internal control object/model is deployment-defined and out of scope.

This keeps the draft implementation-neutral while still preventing unsafe “LLM output -> direct tool effect” assumptions.

comments.author: PieterKas
comments.body: One idea is to use the act claims as described here: https://notes.karlmcguinness.com/notes/standardize-act-across-assertion-grants-and-jwt-access-tokens/

issues.title: how delegation is expressed
issues.author: bc-pi
issues.body:
Relating to https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-klrc-aiagent-auth-00#section-10.2 I think, I had the question, "didn't see explicit recommendation to lean on the act claim to carry agent identity, and the sub claim to carry user identity.  I think we're aligned that this is a fundamental requirement to express delegation, right?" 

I think the answer is "no" but the reasons are somewhat murky (regular old oauth delegation is expressed somewhat implicitly by it being oauth and possibly/probably the client identifier in the access token while token exchange came along later with the act claim). And it might be worthwhile to give a little more explanation in the draft.

comments.body: I got similar questions - also relates to issue #58

comments.author: identitymonk

I like this but I am biased - Author is Amazonian, still it comes from a profiling of a version of transaction token that existed before Transaction Tokens were a draft

see presentation at https://www.youtube.com/live/GbAE49CewYA

comments.author: kevin-biot
comments.body:
I think this issue surfaces a broader interoperability gap: A2A-like patterns can carry identity and invocation context, but delegation for agentic execution needs tighter profiling.

In agentic systems, OAuth is necessary but not sufficient. Token issuance/exchange is plumbing; safe execution requires precise, turn-bounded delegation semantics.

Suggested clarification text:

1. Keep OAuth semantics explicit:
- `client_id`: OAuth client that requested the token.
- `sub`: token subject (resource owner when present; otherwise client principal per profile).
- `act`: current acting party in delegated flows.
- `may_act`: authorization context for actor substitution during exchange.

2. Clarify what this does *not* solve by itself:
- A2A-like invocation context + OAuth exchange alone do not fully define execution-time delegation constraints across multi-step tool/service hops.
- `act` is identity context, not a container for full execution policy.

3. Add delegation profile guidance for agentic execution:
- delegation is turn-bounded (not global/session blanket),
- intent/action scoped,
- audience/resource scoped,
- time-bounded (`not_before`, `expires_at`, max lifetime),
- actor-chain aware,
- non-amplifying across hops.

4. Add default safety posture:
- cross-domain bearer forwarding prohibited by default,
- receiving domain performs local token mint/exchange,
- missing trust mapping/consent/required step-up => fail closed.

5. Keep implementation freedom:
- internal agent/LLM security architecture remains out of scope,
- external authorization invariants at execution boundary are in scope.

This would keep the draft aligned with existing OAuth standards while making delegation semantics robust for real-world, cross-org, turn-by-turn agent execution.

comments.author: kevin-biot
comments.body:
@PieterKas 
Additional context from Internal experience:

We are actively building an agent runtime/governance stack and want to align with RFC-grade standards rather than invent parallel mechanisms. Most of the suggestions above come from concrete gaps we hit in current deployments:

- boundary auth is often clear, but downstream delegation semantics are underspecified,
- turn-by-turn intent constraints are harder than session-level token handling,
- cross-org hops expose trust-mapping and account-linking realities (no universal OAuth domain),
- robust systems need deterministic fail-closed behavior and auditable delegation evidence.

We are not proposing a new auth framework; we are asking for sharper profiling of existing OAuth/WIMSE-style mechanisms for agentic, multi-hop, real-world execution.

issues.author: PieterKas

A comment received via LinkedIn:

I'd also flag one area your draft may want to consider: LLM-specific threats within agents. During development and in the runtime product, I discovered that a single LLM cannot reliably self-enforce safety rules. I solved this with a dual-model pattern
(one LLM generates, a second independently evaluates). The current draft treats agents as opaque workloads _ which is correct at the identity layer _ but implementers will need guidance on the internal security architecture of LLM-based agents,

comments.author: identitymonk
comments.body:
LLM can never be trusted, at the end of the day we want the only element that can make an action possible to be the final line of defense by alwys limiting in space (scopes, authorization details, transaction-oriented nature) and in time (max lifetime, refresh capability and conditions, as long as dynamic revocation - aka SSF)

comments.author: kevin-biot
comments.body:
I would propose  treating internal LLM safety architecture as out of scope for this draft, while strengthening normative external controls.

Proposed scope boundary:
- Out of scope: internal agent implementation patterns (single model, dual model, self-critique, guard-model orchestration, prompt pipelines).
- In scope: authorization/delegation invariants required before any externally visible action.

Suggested security text:
1. LLM output MUST NOT be the final authority for execution.
2. Execution MUST be gated by a deterministic policy/authorization boundary.
3. Delegation artifacts MUST be bounded (intent, audience, scope, actor, TTL) and non-amplifying.
4. Missing trust mapping, required consent, or required step-up MUST fail closed.
5. Cross-domain token forwarding SHOULD be prohibited by default; local exchange/minting SHOULD be used in target domains.
6. Evidence SHOULD bind request, delegation artifact, policy context, and execution outcome for auditability.

This keeps interoperability across heterogeneous agents without prescribing internal AI architecture.

forwarded tokens break least-privilege across trust boundaries.

Main reasons:

Audience mismatch
Token was minted for source domain/API, not target API.
Reusing it cross-domain is authority confusion.
Scope amplification risk
Forwarded token may carry broader scopes than target action needs.
Local minting lets target issue minimum scope for that exact call.
Loss of policy control
Target domain cannot enforce its own auth policies cleanly if it just accepts foreign bearer tokens.
Local exchange forces target-domain policy, consent, and risk checks.
Poor revocation/TTL semantics
Source token lifetime/revocation may not align with target risk model.
Local token can be short-lived and bound to action/turn/tool.
Weak attribution/audit
Forwarding blurs who authorized what at which boundary.
Exchange creates explicit chain: origin delegation -> target token -> target action.

issues.title: Define trust bootstrap and validation rules for OAuth discovery in dynamic environments
issues.author: maheshkukreja
issues.body:
Section 10.9 does a good job explaining what can be discovered at runtime: authorization server metadata, protected resource metadata, client metadata. What it doesn't cover is how any of that discovered metadata becomes trustworthy.

For agents running in multi-tenant or ephemeral environments, this is an attack surface. An agent that discovers an authorization server endpoint and trusts it implicitly is one DNS hijack or metadata substitution away from sending credentials to an attacker-controlled server.

Specific things I think are missing:

- What trust anchors need to be preconfigured before an agent can safely use runtime discovery? For example, does the agent need to know its expected trust domain and issuer?
- Validation rules for discovered metadata: does the agent need to verify that what it got back is consistent with what it expected (issuer identity, key material binding, endpoint consistency)?
- Fail-closed behavior when discovery returns something unexpected or inconsistent: Right now the draft doesn't say what happens if metadata doesn't validate. The safe default would be to reject and fail
- A clear line between what must be preconfigured vs. what may be discovered. The current text treats everything as discoverable, which is fine for convenience but not for security

This gap should also get a cross-reference from Security Considerations (§14) as a named threat: discovery poisoning / issuer confusion

issues.title: Add deployment guidance for intermediaries, gateways, and identity-preserving proxy patterns
issues.author: maheshkukreja
issues.body:
§9.1.1 says proxies, API gateways, service meshes, and load balancers break TLS identity by terminating and re-establishing sessions. §9.2 says application-layer authentication preserves identity across those hops.

What's missing is the bridge between those two observations. In practice, every production deployment has at least one intermediary: a load balancer, an API gateway, a service mesh sidecar; and practitioners need to know who validates what credential and which identity is authoritative after intermediary processing.

There are at least three common patterns that need different guidance:

- Pass-through proxy (transparent TCP proxy): TLS identity stays end-to-end, the proxy is invisible to the identity layer. Simple case, but worth calling out so people know when §9.1.1 concerns don't apply
- Terminating gateway (ALB, Cloudflare, Traefik, etc.): Gateway terminates TLS, possibly validates the agent's Workload proof token / Workload identity token, then re-establishes a connection to the backend
  - The draft doesn't say if the backend trusts the gateway's transport identity, or if it must independently re-validate the agent's application-layer credential
- Token-exchange gateway (API gateway acting as OAuth client): Gateway exchanges the incoming access token for a new one scoped to the backend. The original agent identity may or may not survive the exchange. If the `act` claim (RFC 8693) isn't used, the downstream service has no idea who originally initiated the request. The draft should clarify whether identity preservation through token exchange is a must or a should
- Service mesh sidecar (Istio, Linkerd): mTLS between sidecars gives pod-level identity, but that's the sidecar's identity, not the agent's. The draft doesn't address how agent-level identity (WIMSE / SPIFFE) relates to sidecar identity

I'd suggest either a new subsection under §9 or an appendix that names these patterns and specifies the validation expectations for each. The audit section (§11) should also be extended to say that logs need to capture both the original actor and any intermediary participation - otherwise we can't reconstruct the chain when a gateway was involved.

issues.title: Clarify agent-to-agent interaction and assertions
issues.author: PieterKas
issues.body:
From George Fletcher:

Section 10.5
- Assuming the Agent represents the workload with which the user/human is directly interacting, it makes sense the agent would have the identity assertion. However, in a “turtles all the way down” scenario when the accessed tool is really another agent then that agent will not likely have the identity assertion as passing that along has significant privacy implications.

comments.author: kevin-biot
comments.body:
+1 to this concern. In multi-agent (“turtles all the way down”) chains, forwarding end-user identity assertions by default is a privacy risk.

Suggest clarifying three assertion types:

1) Identity assertion (who) — optional/minimized, not forwarded by default.
2) Delegation assertion (what is allowed) — required hop-by-hop, bounded (intent/aud/scope/ttl/actor-chain), non-amplifying.
3) Execution assertion (target API credential) — obtained locally by the receiving agent via exchange/minting.

Normative defaults:
- Agents SHOULD NOT forward raw end-user identity assertions across hops unless explicitly required and consented.
- Agents MUST forward bounded delegation context for authorization continuity.
- Receiving agents MUST enforce local policy and acquire local execution credentials.
- If required identity/linkage/consent is unavailable, fail closed.

A customer calls to change a flight booking.
They authenticate to the booking platform (for example, via app login + OTP or call-center verification).
That authentication proves who they are to the booking platform, but it does not become a universal token for every downstream system.

When the booking platform needs another agent or backend to perform the change, it sends a signed, bounded delegation artifact: who requested the change (or a pseudonymous subject reference), what change is allowed, which booking it applies to, expiry time, and policy/consent references.
The receiving agent validates that artifact, applies its own local policy checks, and then uses its own local service credentials to call the target booking system (for example, Amadeus).

So execution happens with local credentials, while authority is proven by exchanged delegation evidence.
If trust mapping, consent, scope, or validity checks fail at any hop, the action is denied (fail-closed).

This is the pattern:
authenticate at entry, delegate per action, execute locally, audit end-to-end.

Closed-world, single-organization agent systems are the easy case.
One identity domain, one policy domain, one credential ecosystem: authentication and authorization are tightly coupled, and token handling is relatively straightforward.

Real-world multi-agent systems are different.
There is no universal OAuth domain across all organizations, platforms, and APIs.
So cross-org execution cannot rely on blanket token portability. It must rely on delegated authority.

Authentication is still necessary, but it is only the start.
What matters operationally is precise delegation: explicit, bounded, and context-specific authority for each action.

Delegation must be:

turn-by-turn, not indefinite;
narrow in scope, not broad capability grants;
bound to audience/resource, intent, actor chain, and expiry;
non-amplifying across hops;
fail-closed when any bound is missing or violated.
In short: authenticate once, authorize precisely per turn, execute with least privilege.

issues.title: Multi-party HITL Approval
issues.author: PieterKas
issues.body:
From Jack Lichwa

Regarding Human-in-the-Loop (HITL), I'm concerned that simple human approval via CIBA may not be sufficiently robust for current needs. For high-impact use cases, such as modifying infrastructure like firewall rules or IAM roles, or financial operations like payments and transfers, generic human approval may not suffice across all Human-in-the-Loop scenarios.

For these HITL operations, do you envision a more policy-based approach including specific operation windows, mandatory MFA, and multi-party approvals, similar to Privileged Identity Management systems, rather than just an approved claim through CIBA?

I'm interested to know if you see these granular controls as being in scope, or if you feel these requirements are already addressed through a different approach?

comments.author: PieterKas
comments.body: Regarding human-in-the-loop, it is important to distinguish between the protocols used to request approval (CIBA) vs the authorization policy. This document does not propose what an authorization policy should. A deployment may choose to have multi-party approval, enforced through an authorization server policy. The authorization server may use CIBA to trigger an approval request from each party that should approve the authorization request. We can add this as an example if it helps implementers think creatively about how to use the existing standards. I do not believe this draft should mandate or prescribe specific policies for specific scenarios. Those are risk based decisions for a specific deployment.

comments.author: JLichwa80
comments.body: Thank you Pieter. I think I see the point that document covers how SPIFFE, WIMSE is used for authentication/federation and authorization is handled either locally or through OAuth/Authorization server. I see Agents as malicious due to their nondeterministic nature/like human, versus regular code workloads. In communication like Agent to Agent, Agent to tools/services, both using SPIFFE. federation and OAuth does not seem efficient. I believe I'm looking for similarly decentralized and robust authorization component to glue everything together.

comments.author: PieterKas
comments.body:
@JLichwa80 - the problem is not the one of new standards for authorization, it is about operations and deployment (of existing standards) https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/agent2agent/ixrm8pmAx_CCQ8nfvncG7WHAvT4/

I think we get more mileage out of using what is deployed and known to work, and then make small changes to fill standards gaps.

comments.author: kevin-biot
comments.body:
I agree policy itself should remain deployment-defined, and CIBA is a protocol mechanism, not policy.

But for high-impact operations, interoperability needs a stronger contract for approval evidence. Otherwise “HITL approved” is too coarse across deployments.

Suggested addition:
1. Keep policy choices out of scope (no mandated approval model).
2. Define a portable approval-evidence structure that can represent:
- operation binding (hash of exact action payload/policy snapshot)
- approval threshold (e.g., n-of-m)
- approver role/class and independence constraints (SoD)
- assurance level / MFA result
- operation window (nbf/exp/max lifetime)
- revocation/status at execution time
3. Define fail-closed behavior when required approvals are missing, expired, revoked, or not independent.
4. Add a non-normative example: multi-party approval via AS policy using CIBA per approver.

This keeps the draft pragmatic while making robust HITL patterns interoperable.

comments.author: JLichwa80
comments.body:
Thank you @PieterKas, I think what got me confused : "An Agent, acting as
   an OAuth client, may use the OpenID Client Initiated Backchannel
   Authentication (CIBA) protocol.  This triggers an out-of-band
   interaction allowing the user to approve or deny the requested
   operation without exposing credentials to the agent (for example a
   push notification requesting the user to approve a request through an
   authenticator application on their mobile device)."  - like the Agent have understanding about Authz policy, that it needs to call user for approval. Considering Agent is malicious by default and entire authorization workflow should be delegated to AS, do you mean that Agent does pre-authz? Maybe it is just me that I'm reading that wrong - but I hope you understand my concerns.

issues.title: Authorization layer for AIMS: scoped delegation chains with formal narrowing properties
issues.author: aeoess
issues.body:
The AIMS framework does the hard part right — composing WIMSE, SPIFFE, and OAuth for agent authentication gives you a solid identity foundation.

But as the blog analysis noted, authorization is still an open problem. Authentication tells you who the agent is. Authorization tells you what the agent can do, and crucially, what it can delegate to sub-agents.

We've been working on exactly that layer. The Agent Passport System implements scoped delegation chains with four formal properties:

- Scope can only narrow at each delegation transfer (never widen)
- Spend limits can only decrease
- Delegation depth increments toward a maximum
- Revoking a parent cascades to all descendants

These are the same invariants that IETF DAAP (draft-mishra-oauth-agent-grants-01) arrived at independently from the OAuth side. The convergence suggests these are necessary properties for agent delegation, not design choices specific to any one system.

The interesting integration question: AIMS handles the identity and authentication layer. APS handles the delegation and authorization layer. They compose naturally — AIMS-authenticated identity feeds into APS delegation chains. The gateway enforcement boundary could sit behind an AIMS-authenticated endpoint.

SDK is at `npm install agent-passport-system` (866 tests). Paper on the formal invariants is on Zenodo. Happy to discuss the integration surface if there's interest.

https://aeoess.com
https://github.com/aeoess/agent-passport-system

issues.title: Prior Art: Scope-Based AI Agent Authorization with Power-of-Attorney Credentials (GAuth, patent filed)
issues.author: GoetzGW
issues.body:
Thank you for publishing this draft. The composable approach of AIMS — combining WIMSE, SPIFFE, OAuth 2.0, and AuthZEN into a coherent agent identity and authorization framework — is a well-structured contribution to this space.

I'd like to bring to the authors' attention a body of prior work that addresses several of the same architectural challenges, particularly around agent delegation, scope-based authorization, and principal-agent chains: GAuth (Gimel Auth), developed by the Gimel Foundation.

GAuth's patent application and Github publication predates this draft and covers concepts that overlap with key aspects of the AIMS framework.

Where the overlap exists:

1. Principal-Agent Delegation

AIMS describes identity chaining via draft-ietf-oauth-identity-chaining for user→agent and agent→tool delegation. GAuth formalizes this as Power-of-Attorney (PoA) Credentials — a distinct credential type that explicitly models the legal concept of delegated authority. A PoA Credential defines:

Who delegates (principal)
To whom (agent)
What the agent may do (scope: allowed operations, resource boundaries, time constraints, etc.)
The right to sub-delegate (with mandatory scope narrowing at each level)
This was documented in GiFo-RFC 0110, 0111, and 0115 prior to the submission of this draft.

2. Authorization Policy

The AIMS draft (Section on Authorization) references AuthZEN for policy decisions but notes that the authorization policy layer is "not yet specified in -00 revision." GAuth has a fully specified authorization engine that evaluates every agent action against the PoA scope before execution — not as a post-hoc policy query, but as a real-time pre-action gate. This includes:

Deterministic scope enforcement (allowed file paths, operation types, dependency limits)
Hierarchical delegation chain validation
Instant revocation with immediate effect on all active sessions

3. Agent-to-Agent Delegation Chains

Both AIMS and GAuth support multi-hop delegation (principal → agent → sub-agent → tool). GAuth requires that each delegation level must narrow the scope — a sub-agent can never have more authority than its delegating agent. The full chain is cryptographically verifiable and auditable.

Where Gimel Auth goes further Beyond the identity and token layer that AIMS focuses on, GAuth additionally provides (not limited to):

Architecture Governance — rule-based enforcement of architectural principles (e.g., microservice patterns, MCP protocol compliance, data model approval gates)
Regulatory Compliance Monitoring — LLM-based stochastic compliance scoring against regulatory frameworks (GDPR, NIS2, DORA, EU AI Act), with configurable confidence thresholds
Lethal Trifecta Detection — automatic identification of AI components that simultaneously accumulate autonomous decision-making, critical data access, and execution rights
Plan-Build-Run Lifecycle — formal phase gates requiring architectural approval before an agent may proceed from planning to implementation
These layers operate on top of the delegation and authorization mechanism, which is where the conceptual overlap with AIMS exists.

Request
Given the temporal priority of GAuth's patent filings, open-source licensing and publishing relative to this draft, we believe it would be appropriate for the authors to:

Review GAuth's published specifications (GiFo-RFC 0110, 0111, 0115) as related work
Consider acknowledging prior art in the delegation and scope-based authorization space

We are currently not requesting changes to the technical approach of the draft. We are requesting awareness of prior work that is relevant to the concepts being proposed.

You find the details under Gimel Foundation on GitHub. Happy to discuss further.

comments.author: JayDaley
comments.body:
@GoetzGW I am the IETF Executive Director and this is an official IETF reply.  

Your post appears to be doing two things:
1. Making some form of IPR claim.  The IETF has a long-established policy and process for third parties who wish to make an IPR disclosure regarding IETF work.  This separates the process of IPR disclosure from participating in an IETF discussion or activity.  I strongly recommend that you [file your IPR disclosure](https://datatracker.ietf.org/ipr/about/) so that it can be properly considered by the relevant IETF Working Group .  
2. Participating in an IETF discussion or activity.  As the [guidelines for contributing to this repository](https://github.com/PieterKas/agent2agent-auth-framework/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) clearly state "Any edit, commit, pull request, issue, comment or other change made to this repository constitutes Contributions to the IETF Standards Process".  Please be aware that claiming otherwise has no effect.  Your post above is clearly "acting in order to influence the outcome of a discussion relating to the IETF Standards Process".  

For the IETF policy on Intellectual Property Rights, see [BCP 79](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc8179.html), which is where the phrase quoted above is taken from.

If you have any questions, please contact me directly at exec-director@ietf.org

comments.author: GoetzGW
comments.body:
Dear Jay,

Thanks for your comments — here our response, alongside the previous Foundation correspondence, such as those of 21 March 2026 and 2 May 2026, which have raised the prior art flag under the Gimel Foundation framework.

The Foundation's correspondence has not been on a Contribution footing, and the documentary record is consistent on that point. Across all IETF correspondence to date, one feature has been present in writing in every message: explicit non-Contribution declarations.

We appreciate your guidance on channel-selection. This is why we close this issue, assuming the prior-art message has been received.

We would welcome a constructive working relationship between our two organisations over time.

Thanks and kind regards, Götz
